examples.title: Simple Access Control Check
examples.description: Demonstrates basic access control mechanism checking user permissions before allowing file operations
examples.code:
PROCEDURE checkAccess(user, resource, operation)
BEGIN
    userRole ← getUserRole(user)
    resourceACL ← getACL(resource)
    
    FOR EACH entry IN resourceACL DO
        IF entry.subject = userRole AND entry.operation = operation THEN
            RETURN ALLOW
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    RETURN DENY
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE readFile(user, filename)
BEGIN
    IF checkAccess(user, filename, READ) = ALLOW THEN
        RETURN performRead(filename)
    ELSE
        RETURN ACCESS_DENIED_ERROR
    END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates how ACL-based access control works. The checkAccess procedure looks up the user's role in the resource's ACL and verifies if the requested operation is permitted. If access is denied, an error is returned.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of ACL entries

examples.title: Role-Based Access Control Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation showing RBAC with roles and permissions
examples.explanation: This Python code implements RBAC where permissions are assigned to roles rather than individual users. The ROLE_PERMISSIONS dictionary maps each role to its allowed operations. When checking access, the system verifies the user's role has the required permission.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(r) where r is number of roles

examples.title: Kernel Mode vs User Mode Demonstration
examples.description: C code showing system call interface and mode switching between user and kernel modes
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates the user-kernel mode separation. User applications run in user mode with limited privileges. When they need to access protected resources (like opening files), they use system calls which trigger a mode switch to kernel mode, where the OS performs the privileged operation on behalf of the user.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for mode switch, O(n) for file operation

examples.title: Capability-Based Access Control
examples.description: Demonstrates capability-based protection where processes hold tokens specifying their rights
examples.code:
CAPABILITY RECORD
BEGIN
    objectID: Integer
    rights: SET OF {READ, WRITE, EXECUTE, DELETE}
    expiration: DateTime
    owner: ProcessID
END RECORD

PROCEDURE checkCapability(process, requiredRight)
BEGIN
    FOR EACH cap IN process.capabilityList DO
        IF cap.objectID = targetObject AND 
           requiredRight IN cap.rights AND
           currentTime < cap.expiration THEN
            RETURN VALID
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN INVALID
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE requestAccess(process, object, operation)
BEGIN
    IF checkCapability(process, operation) = VALID THEN
        performOperation(object, operation)
        RETURN SUCCESS
    ELSE
        RETURN ACCESS_DENIED
    END IF
END PROCEDURE

// Example: Creating a capability
PROCEDURE grantCapability(process, object, rights)
BEGIN
    newCap ← CREATE CAPABILITY
    newCap.objectID ← object.id
    newCap.rights ← rights
    newCap.expiration ← NOW + 1_HOUR
    newCap.owner ← process.id
    
    ADD newCap TO process.capabilityList
    RETURN newCap
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows capability-based access control. Each capability is a token containing the object ID, permitted rights, expiration time, and owner. Processes present their capabilities when requesting access. This differs from ACLs as capabilities are held by subjects (processes) rather than attached to objects.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of capabilities per process
examples.spaceComplexity: O(c) where c is total capabilities in system
